1.synthesis: Psalm 141 is a Davidic evening prayer—the “let my prayer be counted as incense” suggests a sunset liturgical context—centered on the petition that YHWH guard the psalmist’s own mouth and heart before requesting protection from external enemies. The opening is urgent: “O Lord, I call upon you; hasten to me! Give ear to my voice when I call to you! Let my prayer be counted as incense before you, and the lifting up of my hands as the evening sacrifice!” (Ps 141:1–2). The central petition then turns inward: “Set a guard, O Lord, over my mouth; keep watch over the door of my lips! Do not let my heart incline to any evil, to busy myself with wicked deeds in company with men who work iniquity, and let me not eat of their delicacies!” (141:3–4). The psalmist then expresses an unusual openness to the correction of the righteous (“let a righteous man strike me”) while maintaining enmity toward the wicked, and closes with a prayer for deliverance from their snares.
1.historical_context: The comparison of prayer to incense and the lifting of hands to the evening sacrifice (141:2) connects the psalm to the temple liturgy’s evening ritual, in which incense was burned and the daily offering was made. The psalm’s inward focus on guarding the mouth (141:3) and heart (v. 4) before the external enemies are addressed reflects the wisdom tradition’s insight that the danger of association with the wicked is not merely social contamination but the gradual alignment of one’s own desires with theirs—“eating their delicacies” is the image of being seduced by what they find desirable. The openness to the righteous man’s rebuke in verse 5 (“let a righteous man strike me; it is a kindness”) is the wisdom tradition’s positive evaluation of correction from the faithful against the refusal to hear from the wicked, whose oil—smooth and pleasant—the psalmist calls an anointing that his head will refuse.
1.literary_structure: Psalm 141 moves from urgent petition (vv. 1–2) to inward protection (vv. 3–4) to the disposition toward correction (v. 5) to prayer against the wicked (vv. 6–10). The structural priority of the inward petition over the outward petition is deliberate: the psalmist prays first for the guard on his own mouth and heart before asking for judgment on his enemies. This sequence reflects the psalm’s understanding that the greatest threat is not always external but may be the psalmist’s own susceptibility to the allure of what the wicked find desirable. The snare imagery of the closing verses (141:9–10)—keep me from the trap, let the wicked fall into their own nets while I escape—frames the prayer for external deliverance as the consequence of the inward fidelity sought in verses 3–4.
1.theological_application: The prayer for a guard on the mouth (“set a guard, O Lord, over my mouth; keep watch over the door of my lips”) is the psalmist’s acknowledgment that speech is not merely a tool he controls but a faculty that requires divine governance. Communities shaped by Psalm 141 take speech formation seriously as a spiritual discipline: the mouth needs a guard because what passes through it reflects the heart’s inclination, and the heart can incline toward evil through repeated exposure and association. The preference for the righteous person’s strike over the wicked person’s smooth oil (141:5) is the psalmist’s discipline in the sphere of truth and correction: faithful wounding is preferable to flattery. The prayer as incense (141:2) frames even emergency petitions as having priestly form—the urgent cry is simultaneously a liturgical offering.
1.christology: The prayer as incense (141:2) is the image of intercession that Revelation applies to the prayers of the saints before God: “golden bowls full of incense, which are the prayers of the saints” (Rev 5:8). Christ is the high priest through whom all prayer ascends: “he always lives to make intercession” (Heb 7:25), and our prayers rise before the Father in him. The guard on the mouth (141:3) is provided by the Spirit who forms speech in Christ’s people: “let no corrupting talk come out of your mouths, but only such as is good for building up” (Eph 4:29). The inward guard on the heart is the work of Christ through his Spirit: “the peace of God, which surpasses all understanding, will guard your hearts and your minds in Christ Jesus” (Phil 4:7).
